enrichment.short_description: AI-powered business intelligence and data visualization platform built on Qlik's associative engine, letting business users explore every relationship in their data, build interactive dashboards, and get natural-language insights without writing SQL queries.
enrichment.pricing_model: Per-user SaaS subscription with capacity-based enterprise licensing; self-managed on-premises licensing also available
enrichment.starting_price: Qlik Sense Business starts at $30 per user per month; Enterprise SaaS starts at $70 per user per month; self-managed Enterprise pricing is custom-quoted
enrichment.headquarters: King of Prussia, Pennsylvania, United States (with a major R and D hub in Lund, Sweden, where the company was founded)
enrichment.seo_title: Qlik Sense Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Qlik Sense in-depth review covering the associative engine, AI-driven Insight Advisor, pricing plans, pros and cons, and how it compares to other BI platforms.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Qlik Sense is a business intelligence and data analytics platform developed by Qlik, a company founded in 1993 in Lund, Sweden by Bjorn Berg and Staffan Gestrelius under the original name QlikTech. Qlik is now headquartered in King of Prussia, Pennsylvania, with a continued engineering presence in Lund, and employs roughly 4,500 people worldwide. Qlik Sense is the company's modern, self-service successor to its earlier QlikView product, and it is built around Qlik's proprietary associative engine, an in-memory technology that indexes every relationship across all loaded data tables simultaneously rather than relying on predefined query paths or joins. This means that when a user clicks a value in one chart, every other visualization on the page instantly filters and highlights related and unrelated data, surfacing connections that traditional query-based BI tools would miss. Qlik Sense is available as Qlik Cloud Analytics, a SaaS product, and as a self-managed on-premises or private-cloud deployment for organizations with strict data residency or compliance requirements. The platform includes Insight Advisor, an AI layer that automatically suggests visualizations, generates narrative insights, and supports natural-language search so business users can ask questions of their data in plain English. Qlik AutoML extends this with no-code predictive analytics, key-driver analysis, and what-if scenario modeling. On the data side, Qlik Sense ships with a self-service data preparation tool that lets non-technical users drag, drop, transform, and combine data from hundreds of native connectors spanning databases, cloud warehouses, SaaS applications, and flat files, with machine-suggested associations to speed up modeling. For technical teams, Qlik also offers a broader data integration and pipeline product line, sold separately, that handles change data capture and ETL at scale. Pricing is per-user and tiered: the Business plan starts at $30 per user per month for small teams needing core self-service analytics, while Enterprise SaaS starts at $70 per user per month and adds governance, larger capacity, and embedding capabilities; self-managed Enterprise licensing is quoted individually and typically involves a minimum one-year, auto-renewing contract under Qlik's capacity-based model. Qlik Sense is commonly compared to Tableau and Microsoft Power BI; its key differentiator is the associative, in-memory exploration model versus the more linear drill-down and query-based interaction common in competing tools, though this comes with a steeper learning curve for new users. The platform is used across industries for embedded analytics inside other software products, enterprise reporting, self-service dashboards for department teams such as sales, marketing, and finance, and large-scale governed BI deployments where IT needs centralized control over data access and app distribution. Qlik Sense supports mobile access through native iOS and Android apps, offline app viewing, and a broad developer toolkit, including APIs and the Nebula.js library, for embedding interactive analytics directly into third-party web applications.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Qlik Sense is the flagship self-service analytics product from Qlik, a business intelligence company founded in 1993 in Lund, Sweden and now headquartered in King of Prussia, Pennsylvania. It replaced the company's earlier QlikView product as Qlik's primary platform for interactive dashboards, guided analytics apps, and embedded reporting.

The product is best known for Qlik's associative engine, an in-memory analytics architecture that maintains every relationship across all loaded tables at once, so any selection a user makes instantly recalculates and highlights related and unrelated data across every chart on the page without re-running a query.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Insight Advisor, Qlik's built-in AI layer, automatically generates suggested visualizations and narrative insights, and supports natural-language search so users can type or speak questions about their data instead of building charts manually. Qlik AutoML adds no-code predictive modeling, key-driver analysis, and what-if simulation for teams that want forecasting without a data science team.

Self-service data preparation lets business users combine, clean, and transform data from hundreds of native connectors spanning cloud warehouses, databases, SaaS applications, and flat files, with machine-suggested associations that speed up modeling. Qlik Sense also offers embedding APIs and a Nebula.js developer toolkit for teams that want to surface Qlik visualizations inside their own products.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Qlik Sense uses per-user, capacity-based subscription pricing. The Business plan starts at $30 per user per month and targets small teams that need core self-service analytics, while the Enterprise SaaS plan starts at $70 per user per month and adds governance, larger app capacity, and embedding features.

Organizations that need on-premises or private-cloud deployment can license Qlik Sense Enterprise in a self-managed configuration, which is custom-quoted and typically carries a one-year minimum, auto-renewing contract term.

features.title: Associative Engine
features.description: An in-memory analytics engine that indexes every relationship across all loaded data simultaneously, so selections instantly filter and highlight related and unrelated data across every visualization without re-querying.

features.title: Insight Advisor (AI)
features.description: Built-in AI that auto-suggests visualizations, generates narrative insights, and supports natural-language search and conversational analytics.

features.title: Qlik AutoML
features.description: No-code predictive analytics, key-driver analysis, and what-if scenario modeling for forecasting without a dedicated data science team.

features.title: Self-service data preparation
features.description: Drag-and-drop data combination, cleaning, and transformation across hundreds of native connectors, with machine-suggested associations between tables.

features.title: Embedded analytics and APIs
features.description: A developer toolkit, including the Nebula.js library and REST APIs, for embedding interactive Qlik visualizations directly inside third-party applications.

features.title: Mobile analytics
features.description: Native iOS and Android apps with offline access to dashboards for field and on-the-go users.

features.title: Enterprise governance and security
features.description: Role-based access controls, data lineage tracking, alerting, and centralized app distribution for IT-managed deployments.

features.title: Flexible deployment
features.description: Available as Qlik Cloud Analytics (SaaS) or as a self-managed on-premises or private-cloud deployment for organizations with strict data residency needs.

pros: The associative engine surfaces data relationships that query-based BI tools like Power BI or Tableau can miss
Insight Advisor and Qlik AutoML bring meaningful AI-driven insight generation and forecasting into the platform without extra tooling
Flexible deployment options span SaaS, self-managed, and hybrid, suiting regulated industries with data residency requirements
Strong enterprise governance, security, and app-distribution controls for large, IT-managed rollouts
Extensive native connector library and a developer toolkit for embedding analytics into other applications
cons: The associative model and broader feature set create a steeper learning curve than more linear BI tools
Capacity-based Enterprise pricing can be difficult to estimate upfront and typically requires a sales conversation
Self-managed licensing and deployment add operational overhead compared to fully hosted competitors
Community size and third-party tutorial content are smaller than for Power BI or Tableau
Entry-level Business plan capacity and app limits can be restrictive for growing teams before upgrading to Enterprise

faqs.question: What is Qlik Sense used for?
faqs.answer: Qlik Sense is used for self-service business intelligence, interactive dashboards, guided analytics apps, and embedded reporting, letting business users explore data relationships and build visualizations without writing SQL.

faqs.question: How is Qlik Sense different from Tableau or Power BI?
faqs.answer: Qlik Sense is built on an associative in-memory engine that indexes all data relationships at once, so any selection updates every chart instantly, whereas Tableau and Power BI generally rely on predefined query paths and joins between visualizations.

faqs.question: Does Qlik Sense offer a free trial?
faqs.answer: Qlik offers trial access to Qlik Cloud Analytics for prospective customers to evaluate the platform before committing to a paid plan; availability and length can vary by region and should be confirmed on Qlik's pricing page.

faqs.question: Can Qlik Sense be self-hosted?
faqs.answer: Yes. In addition to the Qlik Cloud Analytics SaaS product, Qlik offers a self-managed Enterprise edition that can be deployed on-premises or in a private cloud, with custom quoting.

faqs.question: What is the Qlik associative engine?
faqs.answer: It is Qlik's proprietary in-memory technology that holds every relationship across all loaded data tables simultaneously, enabling instant, query-free exploration of both related and unrelated data points.

faqs.question: Does Qlik Sense include AI features?
faqs.answer: Yes. Insight Advisor generates automated insights and supports natural-language search, and Qlik AutoML adds no-code predictive analytics and key-driver analysis.

faqs.question: How much does Qlik Sense cost?
faqs.answer: The Business plan starts at $30 per user per month, the Enterprise SaaS plan starts at $70 per user per month, and self-managed Enterprise licensing is custom-quoted based on capacity.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source hotel property management system built on the PrestaShop e-commerce framework by Webkul, giving independent hotels, B and B properties, and small chains a self-hosted booking engine, front-desk operations, and channel management without recurring license fees.
enrichment.pricing_model: Free open-source core software, with paid premium add-on modules and optional managed cloud hosting sold separately through Webkul's marketplace
enrichment.starting_price: Free to download and self-host; premium add-on modules from Webkul's marketplace are priced individually, generally in the tens to low hundreds of dollars per module, one-time
enrichment.company_size: Roughly 550 employees (Webkul Software, the company behind QloApps, as of 2026)
enrichment.seo_title: QloApps Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: QloApps review covering the open-source hotel PMS and booking engine built by Webkul, its key features, free and paid add-on pricing, pros, cons, and use cases.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: QloApps is a free, open-source hotel property management and booking system developed by Webkul Software, an India-based software company founded in 2010 and headquartered in Noida, Uttar Pradesh. QloApps is built on top of PrestaShop, the open-source e-commerce platform, repurposing PrestaShop's catalog, cart, and checkout architecture to sell hotel room bookings instead of physical products. Because it inherits PrestaShop's plugin and theme ecosystem, QloApps benefits from a mature, actively maintained open-source codebase and a large library of compatible extensions. The core software is free to download, self-host, and modify, released as open-source and distributed through GitHub, where Webkul maintains the active repository and accepts community contributions. QloApps bundles a property management system for handling reservations, room inventory, rates, and daily front-desk operations such as check-in and check-out, alongside a public-facing booking engine that lets guests reserve rooms directly through a hotel's own website. A companion hotel website builder helps properties establish an online presence without separate web development, and a channel manager module can synchronize rates and availability across third-party online travel agencies. Webkul also offers a mobile app builder for hotels that want a dedicated booking app, and a marketplace mode that lets an operator turn a single QloApps installation into a booking platform for multiple independent hotels. Because the core product is free, Webkul's business model centers on a marketplace of premium add-on modules covering functionality such as payment gateway integrations including PayPal, housekeeping management, SEO optimization, virtual property tours, GDPR compliance tooling, guest loyalty and reward programs, and live booking notifications; these modules are priced individually and purchased one-time rather than through a recurring subscription. Webkul also sells managed cloud hosting for QloApps as an optional paid service for hotels that do not want to self-host on their own servers. The platform positions itself around information security and process maturity, citing ISO 27001 certification, CMMI Level 3 alignment, and GDPR- and SOC 2-aligned practices for its hosted and enterprise offerings. QloApps targets small to mid-sized hospitality operators, including independent hotels, bed and breakfasts, aparthotels, and vacation rental properties, that want direct control over their booking technology without paying per-booking commissions to third-party PMS vendors or ongoing per-room SaaS fees. Compared to commercial hotel PMS platforms, QloApps' main appeal is cost: the self-hosted core has no license fee, which is attractive to budget-conscious independent properties and to developers who want to customize a hotel booking stack on open-source infrastructure they fully control. The trade-off is that self-hosting requires the operator, or a hired developer, to manage server infrastructure, security updates, and ongoing maintenance themselves, unlike a fully managed commercial PMS.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: QloApps is a free, open-source hotel property management and booking system developed by Webkul Software, an India-based company founded in 2010 and headquartered in Noida, Uttar Pradesh. It is built on top of PrestaShop, the open-source e-commerce platform, repurposing its catalog and checkout architecture to sell hotel room bookings instead of physical products.

The software is distributed as open source through GitHub, where Webkul maintains active development and accepts community contributions, giving hotels full source-code control over their booking technology instead of relying on a closed commercial PMS vendor.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: QloApps bundles a property management system for reservations, room inventory, and daily front-desk operations such as check-in and check-out, a public-facing booking engine for direct guest reservations, and a hotel website builder for establishing an online presence. A channel manager module can synchronize rates and availability across third-party online travel agencies.

Webkul also offers a mobile app builder for hotels that want a dedicated booking app, and a marketplace mode that turns a single installation into a booking platform for multiple independent hotels, making QloApps usable by both single properties and small chains.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The QloApps core, including the PMS, booking engine, and hotel website, is free to download and self-host with no license fee. Webkul's business model centers on a marketplace of premium add-on modules, covering functionality like payment gateways, housekeeping management, SEO tools, virtual tours, GDPR compliance, and loyalty programs, each priced individually as a one-time purchase.

Hotels that do not want to self-host can purchase optional managed cloud hosting from Webkul as a separate recurring service, while the underlying open-source software itself always remains free to use.

Smaller dedicated hospitality support network compared to established commercial PMS vendors
PrestaShop-based architecture can feel less modern than purpose-built hotel PMS interfaces
Documentation leans technical and is oriented toward developers rather than non-technical hoteliers

faqs.answer: QloApps is a free, open-source hotel property management and booking system built on the PrestaShop e-commerce platform, developed by Webkul Software.

faqs.question: Is QloApps really free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the core PMS, booking engine, and hotel website are free to download and self-host; Webkul monetizes through optional paid add-on modules and managed cloud hosting.

faqs.question: Who makes QloApps?
faqs.answer: QloApps is developed and maintained by Webkul Software, an India-based company founded in 2010 and headquartered in Noida, Uttar Pradesh.

faqs.question: Can QloApps manage multiple hotels?
faqs.answer: Yes, QloApps includes a marketplace mode that lets one installation operate as a booking platform for multiple independent properties or a small hotel chain.

faqs.question: Does QloApps include a channel manager?
faqs.answer: Yes, a channel manager module is available to synchronize rates and availability with third-party online travel agencies.

faqs.question: What platform is QloApps built on?
faqs.answer: QloApps is built on top of PrestaShop, the open-source e-commerce framework, adapted for hotel reservations instead of physical product sales.

faqs.question: Does QloApps include hosting?
faqs.answer: Not by default. The software is self-hosted, though Webkul sells optional managed cloud hosting as a separate paid service.

enrichment.short_description: No-code sales commission and compensation management platform that automates commission calculations, plan design, and real-time payout tracking for RevOps, finance, and sales teams, syncing directly with CRMs like Salesforce and HubSpot.
enrichment.pricing_model: Custom, quote-based annual subscription; no self-serve public pricing
enrichment.starting_price: Not publicly disclosed (Qobra requires a sales demo for pricing; third-party data cites an average contract value of roughly $30,000 per year)
enrichment.seo_title: Qobra Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Qobra review covering the no-code sales commission platform, its CRM integrations, pricing model, pros, cons, and how it compares to other compensation tools.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Qobra is a sales compensation and commission management platform founded in July 2020 in Paris, France, by Antoine Fort, Tanguy Moullec, and Axel Poitral, all alumni of CentraleSupelec. The company builds software that replaces manual, spreadsheet-based commission tracking with an automated, no-code system for designing compensation plans, calculating payouts, and giving sales reps real-time visibility into their earnings. Qobra has raised total funding of more than 15 million euros across a seed round of 5 million euros in March 2022 led by Breega, and a Series A of 10 million euros, about $10.5 million, in October 2023 led by Singular with participation from Revenue Syndicate and existing investor Breega. The company reports serving more than 250 customers globally, including names such as ElevenLabs, Make, GoCardless, DataSnipper, Ogury, and Agicap, with an average contract value of approximately $30,000 per year and roughly 30,000 platform users. At the center of the product is a no-code, drag-and-drop plan builder that lets RevOps and finance teams model complex commission structures, including flat-rate, tiered, and split-commission plans, hierarchical org structures, and multi-currency payouts, without engineering help. Qobra syncs in real time with CRMs including Salesforce, HubSpot, Pipedrive, Microsoft Dynamics, Zoho, Odoo, and NetSuite, as well as data warehouses like Snowflake and BigQuery, so commission calculations recompute automatically as deal data changes rather than through periodic batch imports. On the employee-facing side, reps get real-time commission dashboards, detailed statements, performance tracking, and gamified challenges, plus a mobile app and Slack notifications so they always know where they stand against quota and expected payout. For finance and operations teams, Qobra provides deal-level reporting on margin and cost of sales, forecasting and sandbox simulation tools for modeling plan changes before rollout, role-based access controls, and audit trails for compliance. Qobra positions itself against both manual spreadsheet processes and other dedicated compensation management platforms such as Everstage and QuotaPath, arguing that its real-time CRM sync and no-code plan builder reduce the operational overhead typically required to administer commissions at scale; customers report saving around five days per month on commission management and a 15 to 20 percent uplift in sales performance after adoption. Qobra does not publish self-serve pricing on its website; prospective customers must book a demo or use an ROI calculator tool to get a custom quote, with pricing likely scaled by number of payees, CRM data volume, and plan complexity. The platform holds strong third-party review ratings, including 4.8 out of 5 on G2 and 4.9 out of 5 on Capterra. Qobra is best suited for mid-market to enterprise B2B companies with complex, multi-tier commission structures that have outgrown spreadsheets or basic CRM-native commission tools.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Qobra is a sales compensation and commission management platform founded in July 2020 in Paris, France, by Antoine Fort, Tanguy Moullec, and Axel Poitral, all alumni of CentraleSupelec. The company replaces manual, spreadsheet-based commission tracking with an automated, no-code system for designing compensation plans and calculating payouts.

Qobra has raised total funding of more than 15 million euros, including a 5 million euro seed round in March 2022 led by Breega and a 10 million euro, about $10.5 million, Series A in October 2023 led by Singular, and it reports serving more than 250 customers globally, including ElevenLabs, Make, GoCardless, DataSnipper, Ogury, and Agicap.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: At the center of the product is a no-code, drag-and-drop plan builder that lets RevOps and finance teams model flat-rate, tiered, and split-commission structures, hierarchical org charts, and multi-currency payouts without engineering help. Qobra syncs in real time with CRMs including Salesforce, HubSpot, Pipedrive, Microsoft Dynamics, Zoho, Odoo, and NetSuite, so commission calculations update automatically as deal data changes.

Sales reps get real-time commission dashboards, detailed statements, performance tracking, and gamified challenges through a mobile app and Slack notifications, while finance and operations teams get deal-level margin reporting, forecasting and sandbox simulation tools, role-based access controls, and audit trails.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Qobra does not publish self-serve pricing on its website. Prospective customers book a demo or use an online ROI calculator to receive a custom quote, with pricing likely scaled by number of payees, CRM data volume, and plan complexity.
Third-party data suggests an average contract value of roughly $30,000 per year, positioning Qobra primarily for mid-market and enterprise B2B sales organizations rather than very small teams.

enrichment.short_description: DevOps automation platform that lets engineering teams deploy, manage, and secure applications on their own cloud infrastructure, including AWS, GCP, and Azure, through a self-service internal developer platform, without needing dedicated DevOps expertise for every deployment.

enrichment.seo_title: Qovery Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Qovery review covering the DevOps automation and internal developer platform, its deployment features, pricing plans, pros, cons, and use cases.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Qovery is a DevOps automation platform founded in Paris, France in 2019 by Romaric Philogene (CEO), Pierre Mavro (CTO), and Morgan Perry, with the product launching publicly in 2020. The company builds what it describes as an internal developer platform that lets engineering teams deploy, observe, secure, and optimize applications directly on their own cloud infrastructure, including AWS, Google Cloud Platform, and Microsoft Azure, without requiring every team to have dedicated Kubernetes or DevOps expertise. Qovery has raised approximately $18 million in total funding across pre-seed, seed, and Series A rounds, including non-dilutive financing. Its most recent Series A round, announced in 2025, was $13 million led by IRIS Ventures with participation from Speedinvest, Crane Venture Partners, Techstars, Irregular Expressions, and angel investors including Datadog co-founders Olivier Pomel and Alexis Le-Quoc, Docker co-founder Sebastian Pahl, and Checkout.com CTO Ott Kaukver; the round valued Qovery at a post-money valuation of $45 million. The company reports growing at roughly 115 percent year over year and serving hundreds of client organizations and thousands of individual users. Functionally, Qovery sits between raw cloud infrastructure and application code, automating the provisioning of environments, managing infrastructure-as-code, and giving developers self-service deployment workflows so they can spin up and tear down environments, for example per-pull-request preview environments, without filing tickets to a central platform team. The product includes policy-as-code governance, managed Kubernetes cluster operations, deployment pipelines, and observability tooling, and it has recently added AI agent capabilities, with each paid seat including a corresponding AI agent seat for automating deployment and operations tasks. Pricing is flat-rate and per-organization rather than per-deployment: the Team plan starts from $899 per month and includes 10 human users plus 10 AI agent seats, two managed clusters, up to 100 environments, and 5,000 deployment minutes with business-hours support. The Business plan, Qovery's most popular tier, starts from $2,099 per month and includes 20 users and 20 AI agent seats with single sign-on, three managed clusters, up to 250 environments, 10,000 deployment minutes, a 99.9 percent uptime SLA, and policy-as-code governance. An Enterprise plan offers custom annual contracts with unlimited users and AI agent seats, on-premises or self-hosted deployment options, custom cluster counts, and 24/7 support with a dedicated customer success manager; additional AI agent seats beyond the included allotment cost $10 per month each. Qovery reports that customers see at least a 5x return on their investment through reduced DevOps engineering time. The platform is aimed primarily at engineering and platform teams at growth-stage and mid-market companies that want to standardize and self-serve cloud deployments without building an internal platform engineering team from scratch, and it competes with other internal developer platform and PaaS-style tools in the DevOps automation space.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Qovery is a DevOps automation platform founded in Paris, France in 2019 by Romaric Philogene, Pierre Mavro, and Morgan Perry, with the product launching publicly in 2020. It provides what the company calls an internal developer platform that lets engineering teams deploy, observe, secure, and optimize applications directly on their own cloud accounts, including AWS, Google Cloud Platform, and Microsoft Azure.

The company has raised approximately $18 million in total funding, including a $13 million Series A round announced in 2025 led by IRIS Ventures with participation from Speedinvest, Crane Venture Partners, Techstars, and angel investors including Datadog co-founders Olivier Pomel and Alexis Le-Quoc, valuing Qovery at a post-money valuation of $45 million.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Qovery automates environment provisioning and infrastructure-as-code management, giving developers self-service deployment workflows to spin up and tear down environments, such as per-pull-request preview environments, without filing tickets to a central platform team. It includes policy-as-code governance, managed Kubernetes cluster operations, and deployment pipelines.

The platform has recently added AI agent capabilities for automating deployment and operations tasks, with each paid human seat including a corresponding AI agent seat, and offers single sign-on and a 99.9 percent uptime SLA on its Business tier.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Qovery uses flat-rate, per-organization pricing rather than usage-based billing. The Team plan starts from $899 per month with 10 users plus 10 AI agent seats, 2 managed clusters, up to 100 environments, and 5,000 deployment minutes.

The Business plan starts from $2,099 per month with 20 users and 20 AI agent seats, 3 managed clusters, up to 250 environments, and a 99.9 percent SLA, while Enterprise offers custom annual contracts with unlimited users, on-premises or self-hosted options, and 24/7 support with a dedicated customer success manager.

features.description: AI agents automate routine deployment and operations tasks, included alongside each human seat.

features.description: Automated pipelines connect source code changes to running environments.

features.description: Built-in monitoring to observe application and infrastructure health.

features.description: Single sign-on and an uptime SLA are included starting on the Business tier.

Self-service preview environments reduce bottlenecks on central DevOps teams
Backed by well-known cloud and devtools investors and founders, including Datadog and Docker co-founders as angels
Flat-rate, per-organization pricing is more predictable than pure usage-based billing
New AI agent features automate routine deployment and operations tasks
cons: Entry price of $899 per month is a meaningful commitment for very small teams
Smaller company, roughly 30-50 employees, than major cloud and PaaS incumbents
Requires an existing AWS, GCP, or Azure account, since Qovery is not itself a hosting provider
Enterprise pricing is fully custom and not published
AI agent features are relatively new additions to the product line

faqs.answer: Qovery uses flat-rate, per-organization pricing with the Team plan starting from $899 per month and the Business plan starting from $2,099 per month; Enterprise is custom-quoted.

faqs.question: Does Qovery host my infrastructure?
faqs.answer: No. Qovery deploys and manages applications on a customer's own cloud account, such as AWS, GCP, or Azure, rather than hosting infrastructure itself.

faqs.answer: Yes. Qovery includes AI agent seats alongside human user seats for automating deployment and operations tasks.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source, AGPL-3.0 licensed Ruby on Rails Q&A community platform that powers the Codidact network, letting anyone self-host a multi-community knowledge-sharing site with voting, reputation-based privileges, and Markdown content.
enrichment.pricing_model: Free and open-source software; self-hosted with no license fee (Codidact.com itself is a free community running on QPixel)
enrichment.company_size: Not publicly disclosed (community-run open-source project, not a traditional company)
enrichment.headquarters: Not applicable (distributed open-source community project rather than a headquartered company)
enrichment.seo_title: QPixel Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: QPixel review covering the open-source Q&A platform behind Codidact, its features, license, self-hosting options, pros, cons, and use cases.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: QPixel is the open-source software that powers Codidact, a community-run, Stack-Exchange-style question-and-answer platform. The QPixel codebase originated as a basic Ruby on Rails Q&A project created in 2016, but it was largely dormant until the Codidact effort adopted and substantially rebuilt it starting in August 2019. Codidact itself formed in the wake of a controversy on Stack Exchange involving the removal of a community moderator, which prompted a group of volunteers, led by a contributor known as ArtOfCode, to build an independent, community-governed alternative. After bringing QPixel up to a modern, workable state, the team launched Codidact's first community, Writing, publicly around December 2020, importing an initial set of content from Stack Exchange. Codidact has since grown into a network of multiple topic communities, including areas like software development and writing, all running on the shared QPixel codebase, with the flagship instance accessible at codidact.com. QPixel is released under the GNU Affero General Public License version 3.0, a copyleft license that requires anyone who modifies and deploys the software as a network service to make their modified source code available to users of that service. Technically, QPixel is built on Ruby on Rails for the backend with HTML, JavaScript, and SCSS on the frontend, and it supports Docker-based deployment for easier self-hosting. Functionally, the platform supports multiple independent communities and content categories within a single installation, several post types beyond simple question-and-answer, including long-form articles, a reputation and privilege system that unlocks moderation and site-management capabilities as users build trust through activity, and a vote-weighting algorithm designed to account for controversial or contested answers rather than simple upvote counts. Content is authored in Markdown with live preview, and the platform includes native MathJax support for rendering mathematical notation, making it popular with STEM-focused communities. Because QPixel is open-source and self-hostable, other organizations besides Codidact run their own independent QPixel instances, and the project maintains a dedicated Collab site specifically to help people set up and troubleshoot self-hosted deployments. As a community-maintained project rather than a commercial company, QPixel has no company headquarters, employee count, or traditional pricing; it is free to download, deploy, and modify, with development driven by volunteer contributors coordinating through GitHub, Codidact Meta, and Codidact Collab. QPixel is best suited for communities, nonprofits, or organizations that want a Stack-Exchange-style knowledge base or Q&A platform without vendor lock-in or a per-seat SaaS fee, and that are comfortable either self-hosting the Rails application or joining an existing community on the Codidact network.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: QPixel is the open-source Ruby on Rails software that powers Codidact, a community-run, Stack-Exchange-style question-and-answer network. The codebase originated in 2016 as a small Q&A project, then was substantially revived and rebuilt starting in 2019 when a group of volunteers formed Codidact after a moderation controversy on Stack Exchange.

Codidact's first community, Writing, launched publicly around December 2020 running on QPixel, and the network has since grown to include several topic-specific communities, with codidact.com serving as the flagship deployment of the software.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: QPixel supports multiple independent communities and post types, including traditional question-and-answer content and longer-form articles, all within a single installation. A reputation and privilege system unlocks moderation and site-management capabilities as users demonstrate trustworthy activity, and a vote-weighting algorithm accounts for controversial or contested answers rather than relying on simple upvote counts.

Content is written in Markdown with live preview, and native MathJax support makes the platform well suited to STEM communities that need to render mathematical and scientific notation. QPixel supports Docker-based deployment to simplify self-hosting.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: QPixel is released under the GNU Affero General Public License version 3.0 and is free to download, self-host, and modify, with no license fee. The AGPL license requires that anyone who modifies and runs QPixel as a public service also makes their modified source code available.

Because it is a community-maintained open-source project rather than a company, QPixel has no company headquarters, subscription tiers, or paid support contract; users can either self-host their own instance or join an existing community on the Codidact network for free.

features.description: Content is authored in Markdown with live preview and native support for mathematical notation.

features.title: Docker-based self-hosting
features.description: Simplified deployment for organizations that want to run their own QPixel instance.

features.title: AGPL-3.0 open-source license
features.description: Free to use, modify, and self-host, with a copyleft requirement to share modifications made to publicly run instances.

pros: Completely free and open-source with no vendor lock-in
AGPL license keeps community improvements open and shared
Built-in MathJax support is well suited to STEM and technical communities
Active volunteer development with a public roadmap on GitHub
Multi-community architecture supports several topic areas from one deployment
cons: No official commercial support or SLA, since this is a volunteer-run community project
Self-hosting requires Ruby on Rails operations knowledge
Smaller ecosystem and user base than Stack Exchange
Interface is more utilitarian than some polished commercial Q&A products
Documentation is spread across multiple community sites, including GitHub, Meta, and Collab, rather than centralized

faqs.answer: Yes, QPixel is free and open-source, released under the AGPL-3.0 license with no license fee to download, self-host, or modify.

faqs.answer: QPixel is released under the GNU Affero General Public License version 3.0, which requires modified versions run as a public service to share their source code.

faqs.answer: Yes, QPixel supports Docker-based self-hosting, and other organizations besides Codidact run their own independent instances.

faqs.answer: QPixel is the underlying software; Codidact is the community network and flagship deployment that runs on QPixel.

enrichment.short_description: Self-hosted, open-source assessment engine, released under GPLv2, for building quizzes, surveys, tests, and exams, with multi-tenancy, auto-grading, STEM equation support, and gradebook functionality for schools, training providers, and certification bodies.
enrichment.pricing_model: Free and open-source software (GPLv2 license); no license fees
enrichment.company_size: Not publicly disclosed (small, Canada-based independent software provider)
enrichment.seo_title: QST Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: QST review covering the open-source, self-hosted assessment platform for quizzes, surveys and exams, its features, licensing, pros, cons, and use cases.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: QST, short for Quiz/Survey/Test, is a free, open-source assessment platform built by QST Online Ltd., a Canada-based software provider that first released the software in 2018. The project describes itself as a complete, self-hosted assessment engine designed to give institutions full control over their exam and survey infrastructure rather than relying on a hosted vendor. QST installs on Windows 7, 10, and 11, Ubuntu Linux, and macOS, and it is built on a mod_perl and MySQL architecture that the project states can handle over 1,000 simultaneous test-takers on modest hardware, positioning it for high-concurrency use cases such as scheduled cohort exams. The software is distributed under the GNU General Public License version 2, which means it can be downloaded, run, modified, and even rebranded for commercial use without licensing fees or restrictions. QST supports a wide range of question types, including multiple-choice, matching, true/false, essay, and general survey questions, along with branching logic for adaptive assessments. It includes a built-in WYSIWYG equation editor supporting LaTeX and chemistry notation, making it suitable for STEM subjects where standard web forms fall short. Objective question types are auto-graded, while essay-style responses support manual grading, and results feed into an included gradebook module that lets instructors track marks across QST-based assessments and other assignments with custom weighting, functioning as a lightweight alternative to a full learning management system. A notable feature the project calls Memory temporarily displays study material to a test-taker once, after which they cannot return to review it, intended to test recall or discourage note-taking during an assessment. QST supports multi-tenancy, meaning a single server installation can serve multiple independent organizations, classes, and user groups, which is aimed at large educational institutions running many separate courses and instructors from one deployment rather than provisioning separate hosted accounts per department. The platform supports interoperability through import and export in QST's own XML format as well as Moodle XML, IMS QTI, Word XML, and PDF, easing migration to or from other assessment and LMS tools. Because QST is self-hosted, all student and assessment data stays on the operator's own infrastructure, which the project markets as aligned with GDPR and FERPA privacy expectations, and it reports zero publicly documented security vulnerabilities. According to the project, QST has been downloaded more than 26,000 times across over 185 countries since 2018. QST is best suited for schools, universities, training organizations, and certification bodies that want an on-premises, vendor-independent testing platform, particularly where large numbers of simultaneous test-takers, STEM notation, or strict data residency requirements make hosted assessment tools impractical.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: QST, short for Quiz/Survey/Test, is a free, self-hosted, open-source assessment platform built by QST Online Ltd., a Canada-based provider that first released the software in 2018. It is designed to give schools, training organizations, and certification bodies full control over their exam infrastructure instead of relying on a hosted vendor.

The platform installs on Windows, Ubuntu Linux, and macOS, and runs on a mod_perl and MySQL architecture built to handle more than 1,000 simultaneous test-takers on modest hardware, making it suited to large-scale, high-stakes assessment events.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: QST supports multiple question types, including multiple-choice, matching, true/false, and essay questions, with branching logic for adaptive assessments, plus a built-in WYSIWYG equation editor with LaTeX and chemistry notation support for STEM subjects. Objective questions are auto-graded, and results feed into an included gradebook that tracks marks and weighted assignments alongside QST-based assessments.

The platform supports multi-tenancy, letting one server host many independent organizations, classes, and instructors, and it interoperates with other assessment tools through import and export in QST XML, Moodle XML, IMS QTI, Word XML, and PDF formats.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: QST is released under the GNU General Public License version 2, meaning it can be downloaded, run, modified, and even rebranded for commercial use without any licensing fee.

Because QST is self-hosted, all assessment and student data stays on the operator's own infrastructure, which the project positions as supporting GDPR and FERPA privacy expectations, and the software reports more than 26,000 downloads across over 185 countries since its 2018 release.

enrichment.short_description: Automated tax compliance software that calculates, tracks, and reports sales tax, VAT, and GST for online businesses across more than 12,000 tax jurisdictions worldwide, with automatic invoicing and nexus and threshold monitoring.
enrichment.pricing_model: Tiered monthly subscription based on transaction volume, with a 7-day free trial
enrichment.starting_price: Hobby plan starts at $29 per month
enrichment.company_size: Roughly 13-17 employees (Quaderno operates as an independent unit within Visma, which it joined in 2023)
enrichment.seo_title: Quaderno Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Quaderno review covering the global tax compliance and invoicing software, its pricing plans, key features, pros, cons, and how it fits SaaS and e-commerce businesses.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Quaderno is a tax compliance and automated invoicing platform designed for online businesses, particularly SaaS companies, digital product sellers, and marketplaces that sell across many tax jurisdictions. The company launched in 2014, founded by Carlos, and was self-funded, or bootstrapped, for roughly ten years before joining Visma, one of Europe's largest providers of business-critical cloud software, through an acquisition completed in September 2023. Quaderno continues to operate under its own brand and management as part of the Visma family, headquartered in Las Palmas, in Spain's Canary Islands, with a lean team of roughly 13 to 17 employees. Quaderno's core function is calculating and tracking sales tax, VAT, and GST automatically as transactions occur, drawing on a database that the company says covers more than 12,000 tax jurisdictions worldwide. The platform monitors a business's sales activity against each jurisdiction's registration thresholds and sends nexus and threshold alerts so finance teams know when they are approaching an obligation to register and start collecting tax in a new state or country, a common blind spot for fast-growing digital businesses selling internationally. Beyond tax calculation, Quaderno generates automatic, customizable, and locally compliant invoices and receipts for each transaction, and it offers Quaderno Checkout, a hosted checkout flow that handles tax-compliant billing directly. Multichannel tax reporting consolidates sales tax data across integrated platforms so businesses can file returns without manually reconciling numbers from Stripe, Shopify, or other sales channels. The product connects with common billing and e-commerce tools used by SaaS and platform businesses and provides personalized onboarding assistance to new customers. Quaderno's interface supports at least nine languages, including English, Spanish, Catalan, Basque, German, French, Italian, Dutch, and Portuguese, and automatic invoices are generated in the official language of the customer's country by default. Pricing follows a tiered monthly subscription model based on transaction volume rather than revenue or number of users. The Hobby plan starts at $29 per month for up to 25 transactions and a single user and integration, aimed at very small businesses just starting to sell internationally. The Startup plan is $49 per month for up to 250 transactions with unlimited users, jurisdictions, and integrations; the Business plan is $99 per month for up to 1,000 transactions; and the Growth plan is $149 per month for up to 2,500 transactions, with all plans above Hobby including unlimited users and integrations. Businesses that exceed 2,500 transactions per month move to a custom-quoted Enterprise plan. All paid plans come with a 7-day free trial that requires no credit card, and customers who approach their transaction limit are alerted and automatically upgraded to the next tier rather than being charged unpredictable overage fees. Quaderno is best suited for SaaS companies, digital platforms, and marketplaces that need to stay compliant with sales tax and VAT rules across many countries and states without building in-house tax logic.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Quaderno is a tax compliance and automated invoicing platform for online businesses, launched in 2014 and bootstrapped for roughly a decade before joining Visma, a major European business software group, through an acquisition completed in September 2023. Quaderno continues to operate under its own brand from its headquarters in Las Palmas, in Spain's Canary Islands.

The platform is built for SaaS companies, digital product sellers, and marketplaces that need to calculate, collect, and report sales tax, VAT, and GST correctly across many jurisdictions as they sell internationally.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Quaderno automatically calculates tax across a database covering more than 12,000 jurisdictions worldwide and sends nexus and threshold alerts so finance teams know when they are approaching an obligation to register for tax in a new state or country. It also generates automatic, locally compliant invoices and receipts for every transaction.

Quaderno Checkout provides a hosted, tax-compliant checkout flow, and multichannel tax reporting consolidates sales data across integrated billing and e-commerce platforms so businesses can file returns without manually reconciling numbers from multiple sources. The interface supports at least nine languages, including English, Spanish, German, French, and Italian.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Quaderno uses a tiered monthly subscription priced by transaction volume rather than revenue or user count. The Hobby plan starts at $29 per month for up to 25 transactions, followed by Startup at $49 per month, Business at $99 per month, and Growth at $149 per month, each raising the transaction cap while including unlimited users, jurisdictions, and integrations above the Hobby tier.

Businesses processing more than 2,500 transactions per month move to a custom-quoted Enterprise plan. All paid tiers include a 7-day free trial with no credit card required, and customers approaching their transaction limit are automatically upgraded rather than charged unpredictable overage fees.

features.description: Connects with common billing and e-commerce tools used by SaaS and platform businesses.

pros: Covers more than 12,000 tax jurisdictions in one platform
Proactive nexus and threshold alerts reduce compliance risk
Transparent, published tiered pricing, which is uncommon among tax compliance vendors
Backed by Visma's resources while still operating as an independent brand
Multi-language invoicing suits international customer bases
cons: Pricing is based on transaction count, which can get expensive as volume grows past mid tiers
Hobby plan is limited to a single user and integration
Small team size relative to enterprise tax compliance competitors
Primarily geared toward digital and SaaS businesses rather than physical goods retailers with complex logistics
Enterprise-level needs require a custom sales conversation rather than self-serve signup

faqs.question: What does Quaderno do?
faqs.answer: Quaderno automatically calculates and tracks sales tax, VAT, and GST for online businesses across more than 12,000 jurisdictions and generates compliant invoices.

faqs.question: How much does Quaderno cost?
faqs.answer: Plans start at $29 per month for the Hobby tier and scale up through $49, $99, and $149 per month tiers based on transaction volume, with custom Enterprise pricing above 2,500 transactions.

faqs.question: Does Quaderno offer a free trial?
faqs.answer: Yes, all paid plans include a 7-day free trial that does not require a credit card.

faqs.question: How many tax jurisdictions does Quaderno cover?
faqs.answer: Quaderno's database covers more than 12,000 tax jurisdictions worldwide.

faqs.question: Is Quaderno part of Visma?
faqs.answer: Yes, Quaderno was acquired by Visma in September 2023 but continues to operate under its own brand and management.

faqs.question: What happens if I exceed my plan's transaction limit?
faqs.answer: Customers approaching their transaction limit are alerted and automatically upgraded to the next tier rather than charged overage fees.

faqs.question: What languages does Quaderno support?
faqs.answer: Quaderno supports at least nine languages, including English, Spanish, Catalan, Basque, German, French, Italian, Dutch, and Portuguese.

use_cases.title: Automatic tax-compliant invoicing

use_cases.title: Monitoring economic nexus thresholds

enrichment.seo_title: Qualtrics Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Qualtrics review covering the enterprise experience management platform, its CX, EX, and research tools, pricing model, pros, cons, and use cases.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Qualtrics is an experience management, or XM, software company founded in 2002 in Provo, Utah, by Scott M. Smith, Ryan Smith, Jared Smith, and Stuart Orgill, initially as a market research survey tool built out of a family basement before growing into one of the largest enterprise feedback and experience platforms in the world. The company is now co-headquartered in Provo, Utah, and Seattle, Washington, and employs roughly 5,000 to 6,000 people globally. Qualtrics had a notable ownership history: SAP acquired the company for approximately 8 billion dollars in 2019 shortly before its planned IPO, then spun it back out through a public listing in 2021, before private equity firm Silver Lake, alongside Canada Pension Plan Investment Board, completed a take-private acquisition of Qualtrics in June 2023. Qualtrics' core product is a cloud-based Experience Management platform, launched in its modern SaaS form in 2017, that helps enterprises systematically collect, analyze, and act on feedback across four so-called experience pillars: customer, employee, product, and brand. In April 2024, Qualtrics restructured its offering into three primary suites: Customer Experience, Employee Experience, and Strategy and Research, consolidating what had previously been a larger set of individual products. The platform includes advanced survey design and distribution tools, natively built and statistically validated conjoint analysis, MaxDiff, and choice modeling for market research and pricing studies, plus AI-driven text and sentiment analytics that extract themes and emotional tone from open-ended feedback at scale. Reporting capabilities include customizable dashboards, real-time data visualizations, and role-based reporting so different stakeholders across an organization can track relevant experience metrics. Qualtrics maintains enterprise-grade compliance certifications, including SOC 2 Type II, HIPAA alignment, GDPR compliance, and a FedRAMP-authorized government tier for public sector customers. Pricing is largely opaque and quote-based: Qualtrics does not offer a transparent, self-serve monthly subscription for its professional-tier products, which differentiates it from lighter-weight survey competitors like SurveyMonkey or QuestionPro. The one exception is a published entry-level tier around $420 per month when billed annually, roughly $5,040 per year, for a limited-scope research product. For most enterprise deployments, CoreXM contracts commonly start in the $25,000 to $50,000 per year range for smaller enterprise teams, with median reported annual spend around $28,500 according to procurement data, and full multi-module XM platform contracts, once seats, response volume, and add-on modules such as conjoint analysis or text analytics are factored in, can reach into six figures annually. Qualtrics is used across industries by large enterprises, universities, and government agencies to run structured customer satisfaction and NPS programs, employee engagement and pulse surveys, product feedback loops, brand tracking studies, and academic or commercial market research, typically by dedicated CX, HR, insights, or research teams rather than individual users, given its enterprise pricing structure and administrative complexity.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Qualtrics is an experience management, or XM, platform founded in 2002 in Provo, Utah, by Scott M. Smith, Ryan Smith, Jared Smith, and Stuart Orgill. The company is now co-headquartered in Provo and Seattle, Washington, and employs roughly 5,000 to 6,000 people after a corporate history that included an approximately 8 billion dollar acquisition by SAP in 2019, a public listing in 2021, and a take-private acquisition led by Silver Lake completed in June 2023.

Qualtrics' cloud-based XM platform, launched in its modern SaaS form in 2017, helps enterprises collect, analyze, and act on feedback across four experience areas: customer, employee, product, and brand, and was reorganized in April 2024 into three primary suites: Customer Experience, Employee Experience, and Strategy and Research.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The platform includes advanced survey design and distribution, natively built and statistically validated conjoint analysis, MaxDiff, and choice modeling for market research, and AI-driven text and sentiment analytics that extract themes and emotional tone from open-ended feedback at scale.

Qualtrics provides customizable dashboards and role-based reporting so different teams can track relevant metrics, and it maintains enterprise compliance certifications including SOC 2 Type II, HIPAA alignment, GDPR compliance, and a FedRAMP-authorized tier for government customers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Qualtrics uses a largely quote-based, modular pricing model with no transparent self-serve monthly subscription for most professional use. The one published entry-level tier is priced around $420 per month billed annually, about $5,040 per year, for a limited-scope research product.

Typical CoreXM enterprise contracts start around $25,000 to $50,000 per year for smaller enterprise teams, with a reported median annual spend near $28,500, while full multi-module XM platform contracts, once seats, response volume, and modules like conjoint analysis are added, can reach six figures annually.

features.title: Advanced survey design and distribution
features.description: Flexible survey creation and multi-channel distribution tools for large-scale feedback collection.

features.title: Conjoint, MaxDiff, and choice modeling
features.description: Statistically validated market research methodologies built natively into the platform.

features.title: AI-powered text and sentiment analytics
features.description: Extracts themes and emotional tone from open-ended feedback at scale.

features.title: Customizable dashboards and reporting
features.description: Role-based reporting and real-time data visualizations for different organizational stakeholders.

features.title: CX, EX, and Strategy and Research suites
features.description: Modular product suites covering customer experience, employee experience, and market research needs.

features.title: Enterprise compliance certifications
features.description: SOC 2 Type II, HIPAA alignment, GDPR compliance, and a FedRAMP-authorized government tier.

features.title: Multi-language survey support
features.description: Survey distribution and collection across dozens of languages for global feedback programs.

pros: Comprehensive, enterprise-grade XM platform covering CX, EX, and research use cases in one suite
Statistically validated advanced research methodologies like conjoint analysis and MaxDiff are rarely found in lighter survey tools
Strong compliance credentials, including FedRAMP authorization for government use
Powerful AI-driven text and sentiment analytics at scale
Deep customization and reporting suited to large, multi-stakeholder organizations
cons: Pricing is largely non-transparent and requires a sales negotiation for nearly every tier
Enterprise contracts can run into the tens or hundreds of thousands of dollars annually, out of reach for small businesses
Steeper learning curve and administrative overhead than simpler survey tools
No meaningful self-serve monthly plan for professional use
Implementation and onboarding for the full XM suite can take significant time for large organizations

faqs.question: What is Qualtrics used for?
faqs.answer: Qualtrics is used for enterprise experience management, including customer satisfaction and NPS programs, employee engagement surveys, product feedback, brand tracking, and market research.

faqs.question: How much does Qualtrics cost?
faqs.answer: Most Qualtrics pricing is custom-quoted; CoreXM enterprise contracts typically start around $25,000 to $50,000 per year, while one published entry-level research tier costs about $420 per month billed annually.

faqs.question: Does Qualtrics offer a free trial?
faqs.answer: Qualtrics offers limited trial or demo access for prospective enterprise customers, though there is no broad self-serve free trial for its professional-tier products.

faqs.question: What are the CX, EX, and Strategy and Research suites?
faqs.answer: They are Qualtrics' three primary product suites, covering customer experience management, employee experience management, and market research and strategy tools, respectively, following an April 2024 restructuring.

faqs.question: Who owns Qualtrics?
faqs.answer: Qualtrics is privately held following a take-private acquisition completed in June 2023 by Silver Lake and Canada Pension Plan Investment Board, after previously being owned by SAP and briefly public.

faqs.question: Is Qualtrics FedRAMP authorized?
faqs.answer: Yes, Qualtrics offers a FedRAMP-authorized tier for United States government and public sector customers.

faqs.question: How is Qualtrics different from SurveyMonkey?
faqs.answer: Qualtrics targets enterprise experience management with advanced research methodologies and quote-based pricing, while SurveyMonkey focuses on simpler, self-serve survey creation with transparent published pricing.

use_cases.title: Enterprise customer experience programs
use_cases.description: CX teams run structured NPS and customer satisfaction programs across multiple touchpoints and business units.

use_cases.title: Employee engagement and pulse surveys
use_cases.description: HR and People teams use Qualtrics to run recurring employee engagement and pulse survey programs.

use_cases.title: Market research and choice modeling
use_cases.description: Researchers use Qualtrics' native conjoint analysis and MaxDiff tools to run pricing and product preference studies.

features: Limited scope compared to enterprise suites

pricing_plans.plan_name: Full XM Platform (CX, EX, Strategy and Research)
